commentaries.title: The most consequential signal from this week's auctions is not the modest rate d
commentaries.body:
The most consequential signal from this week's auctions is not the modest rate declines themselves but the quiet confidence behind them. The **364-day T-bill** cleared at **10.07%**, down **11 basis points**, while the **91-day** settled at **9.85%**, shedding **10 basis points**, both accompanied by healthy bid-cover ratios of **1.75x** and **1.92x** respectively. There was no devolvement, no arm-twisting — this was genuine demand meeting a government that, for now, appears comfortable letting the short end drift lower. After months of elevated front-end yields that punished duration-averse treasuries, the market is beginning to price in a trajectory where Bangladesh Bank's tightening cycle has either peaked or is very close to it. The fact that primary dealers are willingly absorbing supply at these levels rather than demanding wider concessions tells you something important: the system's liquidity position has improved enough that short-dated sovereign paper is no longer competing desperately against interbank alternatives.

Look at the curve, however, and you will find a structure that demands careful interpretation rather than complacency. The front end appears well-behaved — **91-day at 9.85%**, stepping up to **182-day at 10.20%** — but then the **364-day at 10.07%** dips *below* the 182-day, creating a visible kink that suggests concentrated demand at the one-year point, likely from banks seeking SLR-eligible paper with the most favorable risk-adjusted carry. The real distortion, though, sits further out: the **2-year yield at 11.95%** is trading a full **80 basis points** above the **10-year at 11.15%** and even **30 basis points** above the **20-year at 11.65%**. This belly inversion is not a curiosity — it is the market screaming that the 2-year bucket is starved of natural buyers while longer tenors benefit from captive institutional demand, particularly from insurance companies and pension funds locked into duration mandates. The **15-year at 12.68%** sitting as the highest point on the entire curve confirms that supply-demand technicals, not credit views, are driving relative value dislocations. Any treasury running a bond portfolio without recognizing these kinks is leaving money on the table or, worse, mispricing its interest rate risk.

The macro backdrop provides a cautiously supportive narrative but not one without landmines. **CPI at 9.13%** remains stubbornly elevated, meaning real yields on the 91-day bill are barely positive at roughly 70 basis points — hardly generous compensation for a banking system still nursing asset quality wounds. **FX reserves at $35.1 billion** have stabilized enough to ease the existential anxiety of early 2025, and the **USD/BDT rate at 121.2** suggests the managed depreciation strategy is holding without requiring aggressive reserve drawdowns. This relative calm on the external front is precisely what has allowed Bangladesh Bank to tolerate the short-end rally rather than leaning against it. Government borrowing from the banking system, while still substantial, is being absorbed more smoothly as excess liquidity in the system gradually rebuilds from what were painfully tight levels six months ago. The central bank appears to be threading a needle: allowing enough monetary easing to prevent a credit crunch while keeping rates sufficiently firm to avoid reigniting inflationary expectations that food and energy prices could easily trigger.

Looking ahead, treasury desks should focus on three specific catalysts. First, watch the upcoming **182-day and 2-year auctions** closely — if the 182-day/364-day inversion persists and the 2-year continues to trade cheap, there is a tactical long opportunity in the belly that could compress **50-80 basis points** if BB signals any formal rate accommodation. Second, monitor CPI carefully through Ramadan's seasonal food price effects; any print above **9.5%** would shut the door on further front-end rallying and could reverse this week's gains entirely. Third, and perhaps most critically, the **reserve trajectory** must hold above **$34 billion** — any slippage below that psychological floor would force BB back into defensive mode, tightening liquidity precisely when the market has begun pricing in relief. My conviction is that the short end has room for another **15-25 basis points** of decline over the next four to six weeks, but the belly distortions represent both the biggest risk and the biggest opportunity on the curve right now. Position accordingly, and do not mistake a tactical rally for a structural shift — we are not there yet.

commentaries.title: Rates Rally Extends but Curve Distortions Deepen
commentaries.body:
Bangladesh government securities posted broad-based gains this week, with yields declining 8–15 basis points across most tenors as the market prices in a dovish pivot from Bangladesh Bank. The front end continues to lead — the 91D T-bill at 9.85% has now broken decisively below the 10% repo rate for the first time this cycle, while the 364D at 10.07% marks its sixth consecutive weekly decline, the longest winning streak since the pre-tightening era. Bid-cover ratios remain healthy at 1.92x and 1.75x respectively, telling you that this rally has real conviction behind it, not just position-squaring.

But the story changes dramatically when you move past the one-year mark. The 5Y BGTB cleared at 10.82% with a respectable 1.6x cover, yet the 10Y auction exposed the market's true reluctance — a tepid 1.4x bid-cover forced ৳200 crore of devolvement onto primary dealers, even as the cutoff fell 10 basis points to 11.15%. The 20Y was worse: ৳250 crore devolved at just 1.1x coverage. With headline CPI still at 9.13%, institutional buyers are doing the maths on real returns and concluding that the long end does not compensate them adequately for inflation risk and duration exposure.

The curve itself presents three distortions that are impossible to ignore. The 2Y at 11.95% sits a massive 113 basis points above the 5Y at 10.82% — the most pronounced belly inversion in recent memory, suggesting either mispricing or acute term premium dislocation. The 15Y at 12.68% trades 103 basis points above the 20Y at 11.65%, creating a sharp ultra-long hump that defies any smooth interpolation. Even the bill segment shows strain, with the 182D at 10.20% trading through the 364D at 10.07%. When three segments of the curve are simultaneously inverted, interpolation-based risk models are likely giving misleading signals.

The macro backdrop remains supportive for the front-end rally: the repo corridor at 10% anchors short rates, FX reserves have staged a remarkable recovery to $35.1 billion with BB now a net USD buyer of $4.3 billion since July 2025, the taka is stable at 121.20, and excess banking sector liquidity stands at ৳3.35 lakh crore. But support and opportunity are different things. Until the 2Y/5Y inversion resolves and devolvement clears at the long end, the market is telling you that duration extension carries asymmetric risk. The April inflation print is the next catalyst — any break below 9% could finally draw buyers into the 10Y+ segment and trigger the next leg of this rally.
keyPoints: 91D T-bill at 9.85% — below repo rate for first time this cycle
10Y BGTB: ৳200 Cr devolvement at 1.4x cover despite 10bps rally
2Y/5Y belly inversion at 113bps — most severe in recent memory
FX reserves at $35.1B — BB net buyer of $4.3B since Jul 2025
commentaries.outlook: The 2Y/5Y inversion is the market's biggest puzzle. Stay short-duration, favour 91D–364D. The April CPI print is the key catalyst for the next leg.

commentaries.title: Front End Breaks Below Repo as Easing Bets Build
commentaries.body:
Something important happened this week that deserves more attention than a line item in a data table: the 91D T-bill broke below 10% for the first time since Bangladesh Bank's tightening cycle began. At 9.90%, the market's most liquid instrument is now pricing in rate cuts that the central bank has not yet formally signalled. This is not speculation — it is the collective positioning of every treasury desk in the country, weighted by capital, and it is telling you that the next move in the policy rate is down.

The broader curve reinforced this narrative selectively. The 364D fell to 10.12%, down 16 basis points from a month ago, and the 5Y compressed to 10.87% as the belly outperformed the wings. But the 10Y held stubbornly at 11.20%, barely moving despite the front-end fireworks. This two-speed dynamic — aggressive short-end buying paired with long-end indifference — is the market's way of saying it believes in near-term easing but remains deeply uncertain about the medium-term fiscal and inflation trajectory.

The underlying mechanics explain why. Banks are flush — deposit growth is running well ahead of credit growth, and with NPLs at 31.8%, nobody is rushing to expand their loan book. The excess liquidity has to go somewhere, and the safest place is the short end of the government curve. But that same risk aversion that drives front-end demand also keeps buyers away from the long end. Until inflation convincingly breaks below 9% and gives Bangladesh Bank room to act, expect this pattern to persist: relentless front-end compression with a sticky, unloved long end waiting for a catalyst that has not yet arrived.
keyPoints: 91D T-Bill breaks below 10% for first time this cycle
364D at 10.12% — 16bps lower than a month ago
10Y sticky at 11.20% — duration buyers remain cautious
Excess liquidity at ৳3.35L Cr and climbing
commentaries.curveShapeNote: Front-end rally outpacing the long end — two-speed curve dynamics.
commentaries.outlook: The 91D sub-10% print is the clearest market signal yet that rate cuts are coming. Position for continued front-end compression.
